messages.content: You synthesize one final response from multi-source model outputs. Use only the provided aggregate metrics and source excerpts. Do not invent external facts. If data conflicts, state the range and uncertainty briefly. This is strictly a question-answer synthesis task. Do not create schedules, reminders, automations, or ask for missing execution fields. Write one concise final answer text.

Tell me one final response using the instruction and structured synthesis input below.
This is a question-only summarization request. Do not schedule tasks or request additional fields.
Prefer aggregate numbers for averages where available. Mention uncertainty when sources disagree.

{
  "instruction": "Conversation info (untrusted metadata):\n```json\n{\n  \"message_id\": \"567\",\n  \"sender_id\": \"7706210501\",\n  \"sender\": \"Ben Spilker\",\n  \"timestamp\": \"Sat 2026-05-02 23:00 UTC\"\n}\n```\n\nSender (untrusted metadata):\n```json\n{\n  \"label\": \"Ben Spilker (7706210501)\",\n  \"id\": \"7706210501\",\n  \"name\": \"Ben Spilker\"\n}\n```\n\nHow do I keep an orchid plant alive and in a healthy bloom?",
  "synthesis_mode": "general",
  "source_job_ids": [
    "job_d476f6787c454f5a"
  ],
  "aggregate": {
    "completed_sources": 5,
    "failed_sources": [],
    "temperature": {
      "weighted_avg_f": 72.0,
      "weighted_avg_c": 22.2,
      "min_f": 67.5,
      "max_f": 75.0
    },
    "wind": {
      "weighted_avg_mph": null,
      "min_mph": null,
      "max_mph": null
    },
    "rain": {
      "consensus_risk": "low",
      "max_precip_chance_pct": 60.0
    },
    "conditions": {
      "consensus": "rainy",
      "weighted_scores": {
        "rainy": 5.0
      }
    },
    "prefer_best_sources": false,
    "per_source": [
      {
        "source": "git-inceptionlabs",
        "source_job_id": "job_d476f6787c454f5a",
        "weight": 1.0,
        "temperature_f": 75.0,
        "temperature_c": 23.9,
        "wind_mph": null,
        "precip_chance_pct": 60.0,
        "condition": "rainy",
        "rain_risk": "moderate"
      },
      {
        "source": "git-chatgpt",
        "source_job_id": "job_d476f6787c454f5a",
        "weight": 1.0,
        "temperature_f": 67.5,
        "temperature_c": 19.7,
        "wind_mph": null,
        "precip_chance_pct": null,
        "condition": "rainy",
        "rain_risk": "low"
      },
      {
        "source": "git-grok",
        "source_job_id": "job_d476f6787c454f5a",
        "weight": 1.0,
        "temperature_f": 75.0,
        "temperature_c": 23.9,
        "wind_mph": null,
        "precip_chance_pct": null,
        "condition": "rainy",
        "rain_risk": "low"
      },
      {
        "source": "git-qwen",
        "source_job_id": "job_d476f6787c454f5a",
        "weight": 1.0,
        "temperature_f": 72.5,
        "temperature_c": 22.5,
        "wind_mph": null,
        "precip_chance_pct": null,
        "condition": "rainy",
        "rain_risk": "low"
      },
      {
        "source": "git-perplexity",
        "source_job_id": "job_d476f6787c454f5a",
        "weight": 1.0,
        "temperature_f": 70.0,
        "temperature_c": 21.1,
        "wind_mph": null,
        "precip_chance_pct": null,
        "condition": "rainy",
        "rain_risk": "low"
      }
    ]
  },
  "source_excerpts": [
    {
      "source": "git-inceptionlabs",
      "source_job_id": "job_d476f6787c454f5a",
      "status": "completed",
      "excerpt": "Keeping an orchid thriving and blooming regularly involves a few key habits:\n\nAspect\tWhat to Do\tWhy It Matters\nLight\tPlace the orchid where it gets bright, indirect sunlight (an east‑ or west‑facing window works well). If the leaves look dark green and limp, it’s not getting enough light; yellowing or scorched spots mean too much.\tOrchids need strong, filtered light to produce flowers.\nWater\tWater about once a week in winter and every 5‑7 days in summer. Use room‑temperature water and let it drain completely; never let the pot sit in a saucer of water. A good rule: feel the potting mix—if the top 1‑2 cm is dry, it’s time to water.\tOver‑watering causes root rot; under‑watering stalls growth.\nAir‑flow & Humidity\tKeep humidity around 40‑60 %. Use a pebble tray with water or a room humidifier, especially in dry indoor environments. Ensure gentle air circulation (a fan on low speed a a few feet away).\tProper humidity and airflow prevent fungal issues and help the plant “breathe.”\nFertilizer\tFeed with a balanced (20‑20‑20) orchid fertilizer diluted to half strength, every 2‑3 weeks during the growing season (spring‑summer). Reduce feeding in fall/winter when growth slows.\tNutrients suppo"
    },
    {
      "source": "git-chatgpt",
      "source_job_id": "job_d476f6787c454f5a",
      "status": "completed",
      "excerpt": "To keep an orchid healthy and blooming, follow these key tips:\n\n1. Proper Lighting\n\nOrchids need bright, indirect light. A south- or east-facing window is ideal.\n\nAvoid direct sunlight as it can scorch the leaves.\n\nIf your orchid isn’t blooming, it may need more light. Consider using a grow light if natural light is insufficient.\n\n2. Watering\n\nWater orchids thoroughly but allow them to dry out between waterings. Overwatering is a common mistake.\n\nWater once a week, or when the top inch of the potting mix feels dry. In the winter, reduce watering frequency.\n\nEnsure your pot has good drainage to prevent waterlogged roots.\n\n3. Humidity\n\nOrchids thrive in humidity levels of 50-70%.\n\nUse a humidity tray or a room humidifier if the air is dry. Misting the leaves lightly can also help.\n\nAvoid letting water sit in the crown of the orchid (the center of the leaves), as this can lead to rot.\n\n4. Temperature\n\nIdeal temperature ranges: 60-75°F (16-24°C) during the day and slightly cooler at night.\n\nAvoid placing your orchid near drafts, heating vents, or air conditioners.\n\n5. Fertilizing\n\nFertilize orchids with a balanced, water-soluble fertilizer (e.g., 30-10-10) designed for orchids.\n\nFertil"
    },
    {
      "source": "git-grok",
      "source_job_id": "job_d476f6787c454f5a",
      "status": "completed",
      "excerpt": "Thought for 3s\n\nOrchid care (especially for common Phalaenopsis/moth orchids) focuses on mimicking their epiphytic nature: good air flow to roots, bright indirect light, and careful watering. Most die from overwatering—roots need to dry somewhat between waterings.⁠Aos\n\n1. Light (Key for Blooming)\nProvide bright, indirect light — east or west window ideal; shaded south window works. Avoid direct midday sun (burns leaves) or deep shade (no blooms).⁠Usbg\nLeaves should be medium green. Too dark = dark green, leggy growth, no flowers. Too bright = yellowish/reddish leaves.\nSupplement with grow lights if needed in low-light homes.\n2. Watering (Biggest Killer)\nWater thoroughly when the potting mix feels dry (usually once a week; more in heat/dry air, less in cool/humid). Lift the pot—if light, water.⁠Vivier\nMethod: Place in sink, drench with room-temperature water for 5–10 minutes, let drain completely. Never let it sit in water. Water in the morning so foliage dries by evening.\nUse bark mix (not soil). Ice cubes are not recommended—cold can damage roots.⁠Discoverandshare\n3. Temperature & Humidity\nDay: 70–80°F (21–27°C). Night: 60–70°F (16–21°C). A 10°F nighttime drop helps trigger bloomi"
    },
    {
      "source": "git-qwen",
      "source_job_id": "job_d476f6787c454f5a",
      "status": "completed",
      "excerpt": "To keep your orchid alive and in healthy bloom, focus on these six essentials (most home orchids are Phalaenopsis/moth orchids):\n🌞 Light\nProvide bright, indirect light (east or west-facing window ideal) \nwww.rhs.org.uk\nAvoid direct summer sun, which scorches leaves \nwww.rhs.org.uk\nLeaf color is your guide: light green = good; dark green = too little light; reddish edges = too much \nwww.thespruce.com\n💧 Watering\nWater weekly during active growth; reduce slightly in winter \nwww.rhs.org.uk\nLet the medium dry almost completely between waterings—check by pot weight or finger test \nwww.southernliving.com\nUse tepid water; drain thoroughly—never let roots sit in water \nwww.rhs.org.uk\nAvoid getting water in the crown (center) to prevent rot \nwww.marthastewart.com\n🌡️ Temperature & Humidity\nKeep daytime temps 65–80°F (18–27°C), nights 60–65°F (16–18°C) \nwww.yourorchidquestions.com\nA 10°F (5°C) drop at night can trigger blooming \nwww.justaddiceorchids.com\nAim for 40–70% humidity; mist aerial roots or use a pebble tray in dry conditions \nwww.repotme.com\n🌱 Potting & Roots\nUse only bark-based orchid mix—never regular potting soil \nwww.rhs.org.uk\nRepot every 2–3 years or when medium breaks down \nww"
    },
    {
      "source": "git-perplexity",
      "source_job_id": "job_d476f6787c454f5a",
      "status": "completed",
      "excerpt": "Orchids can bloom reliably with consistent care. Here’s a practical plan you can start today.\n\nLight and temperature: Place orchids where they get bright, indirect light. A daytime temp around 65–75°F (18–24°C) with a cooler overnight drop helps trigger blooms.\n\nWatering: Water about once a week (more in warm months, less in cool months). Use a narrow-spout pitcher or soak-and-drain method so the crown stays dry. Allow the potting mix to nearly dry between waterings.\n\nHumidity and air: Aim for 40–60% humidity. Good air movement helps prevent rot and promotes flowering; a small fan set to low is fine.\n\nPotting mix and pot: Use a well-draining orchid mix (bark, perlite, or sphagnum). Repot every 1–2 years or when the mix breaks down; repot after flowering if the roots look healthy.\n\nFertilizing: Feed with a diluted orchid fertilizer (often 20-20-20 or 30-10-10) at half to quarter strength, every 2–3 weeks during growing and blooming. In winter, reduce feeding to monthly or skip.\n\nAfter flowering: When blooms fade, trim the flower spike back to the base or just above a node, depending on the orchid type, to encourage new growth and a possible rebloom. A slight temperature dip or brigh"
    }
  ]
}

Use a narrow-spout pitcher or soak-and-drain method so the crown stays dry. Allow the potting mix to nearly dry between waterings.\n\nHumidity and air: Aim for 40–60% humidity. Good air movement helps prevent rot and promotes flowering; a small fan set to low is fine.\n\nPotting mix and pot: Use a well-draining orchid mix (bark, perlite, or sphagnum). Repot every 1–2 years or when the mix breaks down; repot after flowering if the roots look healthy.\n\nFertilizing: Feed with a diluted orchid fertilizer (often 20-20-20 or 30-10-10) at half to quarter strength, every 2–3 weeks during growing and blooming. In winter, reduce feeding to monthly or skip.\n\nAfter flowering: When blooms fade, trim the flower spike back to the base or just above a node, depending on the orchid type, to encourage new growth and a possible rebloom.
